Journal.Title: Medicine and science in sports and exercise

Article.ArticleTitle: Caffeine Increases Core Temperature without Altering Markers of Kidney or Gut Injury after Physical Work in the Heat in Caffeine-Habituated Adults.

Abstract.AbstractText: To test the hypothesis that caffeine intake would increase heat strain and markers of acute kidney injury (AKI), intestinal injury, and endotoxemia after physical work in the heat.

Using a double-blind, crossover design, 14 (6 male) active, caffeine-habituated adults ingested 5 mg/kg of bodyweight (up to 400 mg) of caffeine or placebo 30 min before a 2-hr heat trial (35 °C dry bulb, 50% relative humidity) involving walking (40% V̇O2peak) and box-lifting. Bodyweight was measured pre- and post-trial. Core temperature (Tcore) and heart rate (HR) were recorded every 10 min. Blood and urine samples were collected pre-, post-, and 1-hr post-trial to assess markers of AKI, intestinal injury, and endotoxemia.

Caffeine increased peak Tcore (38.2 ± 0.4 vs 38.0 ± 0.3 °C; p = 0.02), peak HR (147 ± 18 vs 138 ± 17 bpm; p = 0.02) and bodyweight loss (1.7% ± 1.0% vs 1.5% ± 1.1%, p = 0.04) compared to placebo. There were no differences in markers of AKI (urinary IGFBP7•TIMP-2, NGAL) (p > 0.05). Serum cystatin C increased from pre- to post-trial in the caffeine (∆0.24 ± 0.43 mg/L, p = 0.03) but not placebo (∆-0.07 ± 0.17 mg/L, p = 0.81) condition, suggesting a reduction in glomerular filtration. There were no differences in markers of intestinal injury (I-FABP) or endotoxemia (LBP, sCD14) (p > 0.05).

Caffeine ingestion before work in the heat exacerbated increases in Tcore but did not affect biomarkers of kidney or intestinal injury. Workers performing labor in hot environmental conditions should consider that caffeine during work in the heat may increase the rate of Tcore rise.
